speaker: interviewer
text: It is November 20, 1996. The interviewee is Liselotte Ivry Epstein. The interviewer is Lynn Davidow. The city is Montreal. The country is Canada. The language of the interview is English. It is November 20, 1996. My name is Lynn Davidow, L-Y-N-N D-A-V-I-D-O-W. I'm interviewing Mrs. Liselotte Ivry in Montreal, Quebec, Canada. The interview is in English.

speaker: interviewee
text: Tell us your name.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Liselotte Ivry. My maiden name was Epstein.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My first name? L-I-S-E-L-O-T-T-E.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your second name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-V-R-Y.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your name at birth, Epstein?

speaker: interviewee
text: Epstein. Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your birth date?

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you now?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: No I don't really. We did not have any Hebrew names. But I presume that I was Fradl because my grandmother's name was Francisca. So it usually happens that you are named after your grandparents if they are not living anymore. And so it was Fradl.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in what city were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in a small village by the name of Liechtenstein or Listany. That's in Bohemia.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell Listany?

speaker: interviewee
text: L-I-S, with a hacek, T-A-N-Y.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in what country were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Bohemia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me what your childhood was like in Listany.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was really very nice because it was a small village. There were only about 80 houses, about roughly 300 people. We owned a store. My mother did. My father died when I was 3 1/2. And I had a younger brother by the name of Hans, or Jan, or Hanush, depends which country, which language you use. We had a general store. You could buy stockings, and rice, and salami, and petrol. When somebody asked me what kind of a store it was, I would say a small Eaton's. You could get almost anything. And the people came from the surrounding villages to shop in our store. On Sundays they would go to the church, and they would stop first in our store. And they left a list of merchandise they wanted. So while they were in church, we prepared the stuff for them. And after church the women came into the store, and they picked up their, their, their groceries. And sometimes they needed, let's say, stockings or some sewing material. So they would ask my mother to look for that. In the meantime, I was in the kitchen where I was serving drinks to the men. It wasn't allowed, but you know, nobody really looked in. And I was about, I would say, eight, nine. They would mostly drink rum or things like that. I went to school there. It was a very fine school. It had only four classes. So the first class was by itself. The second and third was together. Third and fourth were together. And five, six, seven, eight were together. In Czechoslovakia school was free. It was not by religion. It was a public school. And nobody asked you ever what kind of religion you have. It just did not exist. But, religion was taught in school.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my brother and I, we were the only Jewish children in that village. The priest came to my mother and asked her whether she wouldn't mind that I would attend classes when he is there since he is teaching the Old Testament till grade three. So my mother, she says, that's fine. So I would-- I would stay there when he was teaching the religion. I stayed in that school only till grade six. Because my mother felt that I should go to high school. So I was sent to Plzen where I attend high school. What was your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's name was Elsa. And my father's name was Vicktor.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was she like?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was the most wonderful woman. She was very clever. She was a very good business lady. It was hard for her. After all, she was left with two small children, and she had to make a go of it. But she was a very good businesswoman. People liked her. And she was quite strict with us, which I thought was very good. It, it made a good person out of me. And she had-- she was out of a family of 10 children. She had five brothers and four sisters. And the strange thing was, when ever one of the sister-in-laws or sisters were pregnant and were ready to give birth, they would come to our village, to our town, to our house. And they would have their babies there. So most of my relatives were born in the house where I was born. The house where I was born is a very old-- was a very old house. I was born there. My mother and her siblings were born there. Her grandmother was born there. And her great grandmother was born there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we lived in that village for many, many years. It was-- this village I think goes back to the Hussites. You know, there were wars, the Hussite wars. And they were fighting around there. So there are certain places we knew that they were fighting. So it was a very old village.

speaker: interviewee
text: The people in the village spoke German, because that's before Czechoslovakia. It was Austria-Hungary. And they were mostly farmers. When I tell people how I grew up, they thought that I must live, I don't know, in the Middle Ages. Because we did not have any electricity. We did not have a bathroom. We didn't have-- we had to go to an outhouse. But you know, when you grew up like that, and everything was nice, and the people were nice, and the countryside was most beautiful. We had beautiful little forests. And in the fall I would go in the forest and pick mushrooms. 5 o'clock in the morning my friend and I would go all by ourselves. And really nobody worried that something was going to happen to us. During the summer we went to pick blueberries and strawberries. It was wonderful. It was just such a nice little village.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your other activities, like sports? What did you enjoy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we played soccer. Maybe called it football. And just skipping and running. In winter we were doing sleigh riding. My mother had a little red sleigh. And I used to take it to school and parked it in front of the school house. And at noon, we would go home for our lunch. So I just would take my little sleigh, and we went down near the pond. And I would ride down. It was-- that was just an everyday occurrence. It never changed. In front of our house there was a pond. And in winter it froze because it was quite cold where I lived And so I wanted to go-- I needed some skates. So my mother had an old pair of skates, probably from the turn of the century. They were made out of iron. And I had to screw them onto my boots. And of course, by the time I came to the pond, they fell of about three times. But anyway, I managed. It wasn't the greatest thing, but I still could do it. We swam also in summer in that pond. The geese, and the ducks, and the horses, and the cows, and the children, we all were in the pond. And so it really was wonderful.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you like to paint or draw?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I always did. I always had-- no piece of paper was safe from me because I always was doodling and doing something like that. And I also submitted some of-- I like to do handicrafts. I-- we learned in school, in grade two, we learned how to crochet, and how to knit, and how to sew. And I would do very good work. One day I asked my mother when she went Plzen to do her shopping for the store to bring me some wool So she brought me wool. I remember like today. It was beautifully coral red. And I would make a sweater just by myself. I could knit it all by myself. It had a pattern. And nobody showed me how. And I just did it because I was very good at that. Even till now I do all this type of thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have anything to do with the farmers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, with the farmers, we used to help bring in the crop. And the big farms, they had a threshing machine. It was by steam. And a friend of mine, her mother was the one who was loading the machine. And the best thing was, of course, at 10 o'clock when they stopped. And the farmers' wives, they would give us these huge loaves of bread, and fresh butter, and fresh cheese. And everybody had a wonderful time. In the back of the farms there usually were gardens. And In the gardens you had all the fruit you wanted-- pears, and apples, and apricots, and plums, and everything. We didn't have a well. So we took the water from our neighbor. And for that, we had to work two days in the field. It was potato days. So we helped them bring in the potatoes. It was very nice because at the end of the day, we would take the potatoes. We would make a bonfire. And we would bake the potatoes and had a real good time.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to ask you what you know about your father, what you heard of him.

speaker: interviewee
text: I really didn't know my father very well at all. Because I only was 3 and 1/2 years old. I do remember him slightly. But when I came to Canada, my uncle, who was the youngest brother of his, he would tell me that he was a very strong man. His parents owned an inn. And perhaps later I can show you a picture of my father with a broken nose. There was a drunk there, and he took him and threw him out. And the drunk punched him in the nose. And so he had a broken nose, but it was never fixed. He was in the First World War. So were all my other relatives-- men relatives, of course. And so they only got married after the war. It was very hard for young Jewish people to get together because it wasn't like, let's say, in Poland where the villages and towns where there are mostly Jewish people. Where we lived we were only four Jewish families. And maybe in the next town also were only four Jewish families. So what they did, they made balls where they invited all the Jewish young people. And that's where they met. And that's how they got married. And so my mother and father got married in 1920 when my father came home from the war. And he came home in 1920.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he win any medals?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, he had some medals. I don't know what kind they were, probably for bravery. He must have been a very brave man.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a synagogue nearby?

speaker: interviewee
text: In our village there was no synagogue. There was a synagogue not too far. And then there were not too many people. So we moved to another synagogue. It was called Cherubi. That's where we went to synagogue. And of course, we walked. And let's say, on Yom Kippur, at the end, our driver would come with the little carriage and the horses and would take us home.

speaker: interviewer
text: How often could you go to synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: We only went on high holidays because during the year there were no services. We did not have enough people to come to services.

speaker: interviewer
text: At home did you have a favorite holiday, a Jewish holiday?

speaker: interviewee
text: We celebrated all the holidays. I think the most memorable one was probably Pesach. We did-- we kept all the holidays. We had matzot. There was a place in the town of Pardubice, it was called, where they baked the matzo. And they were like here. They had the shmurah matzo, like big round ones. But they weren't as burnt as a shmurah matzo. And as a matter of fact, my mother kept-- they came in five kilo bags. And she would keep one in the store. And every customer would get one piece of matzah. Since we did not have a man in the house, we would go to our neighbor, a Mr. Sunderstein, who would conduct the Seder. And I must tell you that we did not eat chicken on Passover. We ate kid, just like it says in the Haggadah. And then when Mr. Sunderstein died, so there was no one in the village who could conduct the Seder. So we went to Plzen, which is about 18 kilometers from Lichtenstein or Listany. So again, our driver would take the, the wagon with the horses and would take us there. And then in the middle of the night we would drive back. You know, here in Canada, or in North America, it's not such a thing that people really have to put themselves out to do certain things for the holidays. When you lived in a small place like I did, it wasn't so easy. We lived on matzot, potatoes, lots of eggs, lots of goose fat, not much meat. But it didn't really matter. But everybody in the village knew that we had a holiday.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother have a special dish she liked to prepare?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, she had many dishes. And as a matter of fact, I asked some people, older women, here whether they knew. No, because you see, each country has its own specialty. And so, for instance, I did not know anything about gefilte fish. We did not have any fish because in Bohemia there are only ponds. And we didn't have many very often fish. So we did not know gefilte fish. I did not know many other dishes what people-- for instance kishke, we never did that. We had different things. We also didn't call challah challah. We called it barches. It comes from Bracha, so we called it barches.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go after Listany?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I-- when my mother sent me to school, to Plzen, I only stayed there one year. That was 1937, 1938. And I, I was a boarder with a family. And I didn't have my own room. I, I slept with the other people. But, you know, we never thought of it, that it was funny. Now I look back at it and I thought that really wasn't that nice. But at that time I really didn't care. So I stayed there for one year. And every Friday night we would go visiting some people of theirs, their friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the discussion was always what's going to be-- because it was 1937, and Hitler already started doing mischief. And so the people were already discussing it. And since I was quite young, but it disturbed me. Why would they always talk about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it didn't take too long. It was 1938, and it was already after Hitler went into Poland and into Austria. He came into the Sudentenland. That was a border land of Bohemia. And he started occupying. The first places were occupied from the first of October 1938. And our place wasn't supposed to be occupied. But somehow they took it in on the eighth of October. During the night we heard-- there was a garrison of soldiers. Because, you see, at that time, people thought that there's going to be a war between Czechoslovakia and Germany. So there was a garrison of soldiers. And my mother asked them if there's anything whether they would help us. So in the middle of the night they came. They needed petrol. They woke her up, and she gave them petrol for their trucks and so on. And she asked them whether they would take us along. And they said they would.

speaker: interviewer
text: So a truck came. And they took some of our things from the store. And my mother asked them whether they first would take me along. So she put me in a truck with the soldiers. And she told them that they should take me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now if you think about it today, at-- yes, I was 13 at that time-- would go with the soldiers in the middle of the night. I mean, you would never think of it. But we did. At that time you didn't think of any bad things.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they took me to some relatives in Chemotehna. And of course, when I arrived they were so overwhelmed. How is it possible that I am arriving? And so they told her-- told them that we are running away and they're bringing us and that my mother and brother would come with the next truck. So they did. And my mother was very distraught because we had to leave everything. And we just ran. We didn't have a cup, nothing, absolutely nothing, just some clothes. And we took some things from the store.

speaker: interviewee
text: The soldiers put the, the merchandise in a warehouse. But unfortunately, at night some of them returned and stole most of it. We knew that we couldn't stay with the relatives. They didn't have room. And my mother wanted to go to Prague because there she had-- most of her brothers and sisters lived there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we left there. And we arrived on the 28th of October 1938 in Prague. Coincidentally, the 28th of October is the date Czechoslovakia was declared a country in 1918. So we went to some relatives. And other relatives arrived. And they said, Lisel is going to go with this one. And Hansel is going to go with this one. And Elsa is going to stay with her sister Helena. And so this happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I stayed with an uncle, Richard, and his wife, Hilda. She was very strict. And she used to be a school teacher. And so she said that I have to start school right away. And I had to go to a Czech school. I only spoke German. My mother hired a teacher to teach me Czech, but it-- you know, it wasn't really very much. So she went with me to school. And she told them that here is this child, and she wants that I should go there to school. So I started school. She found a young girl who was a bright girl. And she helped me with grammar and things like that. At the end of the school year I spoke through fluently Czech.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you belong to any youth organizations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at that time. That was later. So the school year '38, '39 was disrupted because on the 15th of March 1939, the Germans walked into Prague. And so '38, '39 was my last school year. After that we were not allowed to go to school. But my mother again was very ingenious. She found a school for me. It was called the Youth Aliyah school to prepare us to go to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went there for two years, where I studied all the regular subjects-- also sewing, pattern making, Hebrew, and English. And that was supposed to help us when we get to Israel to live in a kibbutz and to be active people there. Unfortunately, it didn't work out that way.

speaker: interviewee
text: We come to 1940, '41. Things were not so very good anymore in Prague. Of course, we were not allowed to go to school, as I said. We were not allowed to go to a park. We were not allowed to go to a movie house. We were not allowed to be on a sidewalk. We were not allowed to shop in only certain hours. And every day there was something new. You know, you would go out on the street, and they had it was called Kundmachung. That means they wanted to let you know what the new laws would be. So you had to read what they wanted us to know. We also had to give up, in case somebody had a fur coat or jewelry, everything had to be given. Radios was the first thing so we couldn't listen to the outside world. And we heard that they're going to have transports to empty out Prague of all the Jews. So in the fall of '41, three transports were sent to Lodz. And they took first the very poor, and then the rich and the poor. But by that time, Eichmann already had a plan to rid the whole of Europe of the Jews. And they had to start somewhere. So they found a town by the name of Theresienstadt. It's named after the Empress Maria Theresa, or in Czech it's called Theresien, where they sent 1000 young men. It was called [NON-ENGLISH] to prepare the town for the rest of us. This town was an armory, but it had a support group of people living there. There were tailors, all the people who need to be in a town. And they had to find places where they were going to move because they wanted to empty [? out ?] the town And bring all the Jewish people from Bohemia and Moravia to Theresien.

speaker: interviewer
text: So [NON-ENGLISH] prepared everything. They made cots and, of course, whatever needed to be done. So the first transports started to go. And it usually was 1000 people. And they would get a letter that they had to be in this and this place at this and this time and that they only could have one suitcase and things like that. So we were living there. It was a very unsettling time. Even so, we were young, and you wanted to have other ideas about your future than going where we really didn't know. Well, what did you want to be when you grew up? Did you have special dreams?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I tell you, it's-- dreams-- at that time, people really couldn't have many dreams. But I did want to be an artist or perhaps someone who studied many languages because I have a very good ear for languages. And it doesn't take me long to pick up a language. But we didn't really dream. We lived from day to day, even at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like for you when you came to Prague? You came from such a small village, and then you came to this cosmopolitan city.

speaker: interviewee
text: It didn't make really much difference to me because it was-- it was-- it was a very hard time. Even if I would have gone to a small town, leaving home, living with relatives who had to put up with me, it wasn't something they really looked forward to. They were already-- at that time I thought they were old. Of course, they weren't. But here they had a grown up daughter who was married. And here I was, and there were certain laws that I had to be home at 8 o'clock. And at that time I already joined a Zionist organization. And we had meetings. And perhaps I like to be there. And it was 5:00 to 8:00, and I had to be home. And I started running like crazy to be home on time that nobody should catch me. And I would arrive at my, my aunt's apartment. Of course it was my poor uncle opened the door, and he had to be nasty to me because she told him that I shouldn't be late and that I put them in jeopardy because I live with them. And you know, you have to be home at 8:00, and they could have caught me and who knows what. So it was an-- it was very unsettling.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the Zionist group you belonged to?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was called El Al. It was-- it wasn't-- it was like the Social Democrat. It wasn't too much to the left, and it wasn't too much to the right. The El Al people, when they went to Israel, they lived on a kibbutz, but not like Hashomer Hatzair, where you really didn't own anything. Whereas in the kibbutz where El Al went, you did have some of your own things.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the family that you stayed with?

speaker: interviewee
text: My uncle and my aunt?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sicher. That's my mother's maiden name. And the uncle I stayed with was Richard Sicher. He was the oldest brother of my mother. And his wife was Hilda. And she was like a major general. But I think that she really meant well with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you encounter a lot of anti-Semitism at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe how you were taken to Theresien?

speaker: interviewee
text: I want to tell you-- you asked me about anti-Semitism. In Prague the Czech people had problems also because after all, the Germans didn't like the Czechs. So they themselves were very-- they couldn't do exactly what they wanted. And it was-- at that time, Czechoslovakia was one of the most democratic countries in the world. There was nothing that you couldn't do. It was such a free country. And then being overrun by the Germans, it was-- for everyone, it was difficult.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you hear of any Aktions taken where you were?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the Aktion that we had to leave. You know, people went to-- people went on transports. They went once a week, perhaps. And the transports were numbered, or rather lettered. It starts A-B-C-D. And then it goes a AA, AB, AC, and then AAB, AAC. My transport number of was BF992. And I went to Theresien in September in '42, at the beginning of September.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll take a little break now, Mrs. Ivry.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
